message:
fix(rchd): swap `Instant::now() - window` for `saturating_duration_since` (fresh-boot panic)

Two sites in rchd computed a time-window cutoff by subtracting a
`Duration` from `Instant::now()`:

  - cancellation.rs::compute_operator_debt — pruning
    recent_cancellations / recent_escalations / recent_cleanup_failures
    against a DEBT_WINDOW-old cutoff.
  - selection.rs::recent_selections + SelectionHistory::prune —
    counting / pruning worker-selection history against a rolling window.

`Instant::now() - Duration` panics in Rust if the result would precede
the monotonic clock's origin. On Linux the monotonic clock starts at
zero at boot, so on a freshly-booted host where `Instant::now()` is
smaller than `DEBT_WINDOW` (or the callers' selection window), the
subtraction underflows and panics — which, under rchd, means the
daemon would crash on its first cancellation-debt query or first
selection-history read after boot. On a typical rchd deployment that's
a narrow window but a reproducible panic.

Fix: compare `now.saturating_duration_since(t) < window` (or `>=` for
the prune loop) instead of constructing a cutoff `Instant`. The
saturating form returns Duration::ZERO when `t` is after `now` (which
in this code path can't happen, but is a defined no-panic answer
anyway) and behaves identically to the subtraction form for the
post-boot steady-state case. Both sites get a same-shape rewrite so
they remain symmetrical.

Also updates the comment at each site to explicitly call out the
fresh-boot underflow hazard, so a future author doesn't naïvely revert
to the shorter-looking subtraction form.

No new tests — the panic is environment-dependent (requires booting on
a clock where `now < window`) and contorting the test harness to fake
a low monotonic clock is worse than the inline documentation of the
invariant. Existing selection_history / cancellation tests continue to
cover the windowing semantics.
